data.title: Expand Energy: Buy On The Post-Earnings Dip
data.description:
Expand Energy: Buy on Post-Earnings Dip

Expand Energy Corporation (EXE) just put up one of its strongest quarters, and somehow the stock is cheaper today than before earnings.

The company's net income came in at $553 million and operating cash flow at $956 million on production of 7.4 Bcfe/d. Full-year adjusted EBITDAX hit $5.1 billion. These are big numbers for a stock that the market seems to be discounting.

I see this as an excellent entry point for investors, however.

Shares have pulled back roughly 14% from their 52-week high of $126.62, dragged down by news of its CEO's departure on February 9 and a $0.75/Mcf slide in the gas strip since late November. I think the sell-off is overdone here.

The reasons for its dip—CEO departure and softer gas strip prices—are all temporary. None of them changes their strong operations or the natural gas demand story (which has only improved and will continue to improve), and this kind of dislocation is when long-term investors build positions.

On the Q4 earnings call, Interim CEO Mike Wichterich laid out a natural gas demand picture that should make every energy investor pay attention: “We are seeing 35% to 40% [demand growth] in the next five years,” with SVP Daniel Turco adding, “We are seeing about 25 billion cubic feet a day of gas demand coming online” from LNG, power, and industrial markets.

I'm starting coverage on Expand Energy with a BUY rating and a $150 price target, and I explain why below.

Expand Energy: What the Earnings Report Confirms

The Q4 earnings release was good by any measure.

Net production of 7.4 Bcfe/d was up 15% year-over-year, with its gas mix at 92%. Adjusted EPS of $2.00 beat the consensus of $1.88. Operating cash flow came in at $956 million, which is more than double what they did in Q4 2024.

For the full year, we're looking at $1.82 billion in net income, $5.08 billion in adjusted EBITDAX, and $4.58 billion in operating cash flow. On top of that, Expand returned $865 million to shareholders while still knocking $660 million off gross debt. That's a management team doing what they said they'd do.

And it's setting up to be a strong 2026, too. Expand is targeting output of 7.5 Bcfe/d off of capex of $2.85 billion.

The CEO, Wichterich, chimed in on operational execution on the company earnings call. “We have a 15% reduction in our breakevens in the Haynesville. That is very difficult to do. It doesn't just help our reinvestment rate. It also helps our inventory.”

COO Josh Viets added the company has “been able to add five years of inventory below $3.50” in a single year, expanding their economic drilling runway even at lower gas prices.

This next point might be the most underappreciated part of the story. Management formally outlined a $0.20/Mcf improvement target on realized gas prices, confirming roughly $500 million in incremental EBITDA.

Leadership Transition and Houston Relocation

On February 9, CEO Nick Dell’Osso stepped down related to the company's relocation to Houston, and Chairman Michael Wichterich took the Interim CEO role.

Dell’Osso deserves credit, and it's bittersweet to see him go. After all, he grew EXE from a $5 billion business to $26 billion.

The market didn’t like the news. Shares dropped nearly 6%, which I think was a knee-jerk reaction.

Wichterich addressed the elephant in the room head-on: “Call it six [months] is the goal [to find a replacement CEO]. I wouldn't be surprised if it went to nine months. I am committed to find the right person. I will be here until that occurs.”

The Houston relocation announcement is the other piece people are underweighting. Wichterich was blunt: “Moving to Houston is to be in that room, and so we have to get out of our own way.”

Houston is where the LNG deals happen and where the capital markets live. I don't see the relocation or CEO departure as negatives for the stock long-term; rather, as short-term blips.

AI and Data Center Demand

Here’s where the story gets really interesting for Expand Energy.

The IEA projects U.S. data center electricity demand will more than double from 183 TWh in 2024 to roughly 426 TWh by 2030, accounting for nearly half of all U.S. electricity demand growth.

Also, the DOE estimated 100 GW of new peak capacity is needed by 2030, with half from data centers, requiring 10–20% more U.S. gas production.

On the conference call, Turco connected this directly to Expand’s positioning: “Half of that [demand growth] is coming from LNG, and that sits right in our backyard...right where our Haynesville asset is.”

Viets added that “the inventory that we carry in the Haynesville is just simply unmatched...both in terms of depth and quality.” They’re in the right basin at the right time.

LNG Exports Add a Second Structural Demand Driver

The U.S. is building out a massive wave of new LNG export capacity. Total capacity is expected to jump from about 15.5 Bcf/d today to over 25 Bcf/d by 2028, with five projects already under construction adding 9.7 Bcf/d. That kind of buildout has real pricing implications: Henry Hub is forecast to roughly double from $2.20/MMBtu in 2024 to around $4.50 in 2026.

When asked on the call where all this demand is coming from, Wichterich didn't try to narrow it down: “LNG is definitely manufacturing. It is power. I think it is all. And so all of the above.”

For Expand Energy, with deep Haynesville inventory sitting right next to Gulf Coast LNG terminals, this is about as direct a beneficiary as you'll find in the gas space.

Focused on Improving the Balance Sheet

The balance sheet is in great shape: $613 million in cash, a $3.5 billion revolver extended to 2030, and gross debt down by $1.25 billion since the merger, with a target of at least $1 billion more coming off in 2026.

The $0.575/share quarterly dividend ($2.30 annualized) marks 20 consecutive quarters of payments.

Expand Energy: The Valuation

Expand Energy carries a forward P/E of 11.16 currently, according to YCharts, which is well below some of its natural gas peers listed above.

The stock also trades at an EV/EBITDA of just 4.75 (on a forward basis), according to Seeking Alpha. You could certainly make the case here that the stock is cheap.

I don’t see any reason why its shares should be trading at a discount to its peers. If anything, I think the stock needs to trade at a slight premium. This is North America’s largest gas producer, sitting at the center of a structural demand wave from AI and data centers.

Risks

The risks to owning Expand are the same as other natural gas stocks. Mainly, a substantial drop in natural gas prices below $3.00/Mcf would certainly hurt its cash flow and potentially lead to smaller dividends and less share buybacks (not to mention a lower stock price).

The CEO replacement overhang might continue to weigh on shares, too. The company needs to find a good replacement sooner rather than later.

There's also potential M&A risk. Wichterich noted in the conference call that the company “looked at a lot of transactions” in 2025 and “passed on a lot,” which I think shows discipline, but M&A execution risk is always present. Hopefully, the company will continue to be selective and only pursue opportunities with a clear ROI for Expand.

These are all real concerns, but I believe they're all manageable as well.

Future Catalysts to Watch

Pay attention to these key catalysts expected in 2026.

  • A permanent CEO appointment within six to nine months, removing the leadership overhang (I would guess they find a new CEO within 6–7 months).
  • Realization of the $0.20/Mcf gas marketing uplift, which would be worth roughly $500 million in incremental EBITDA, according to the company.
  • Continued LNG export capacity buildout, with five projects under construction adding 9.7 Bcf/d.
  • Accelerating AI and data center power demand from hyperscaler capital spending.
  • At least $1 billion in additional debt reduction in 2026, based on the company's earnings call comments.
  • Natural gas strip improvement as 25 Bcf/d of structural demand growth absorbs supply.
  • Potential increase in shareholder returns as debt targets are met.
  • Potential M&A.

Expand Energy: The Bottom Line

Expand Energy is North America’s largest natural gas producer. The company just delivered a strong 2025. It’s paying down debt aggressively, chasing a $500 million EBITDA uplift through smarter gas marketing, and relocating to Houston as a strategic, long-term move.

With a 7x trailing EV/EBITDA with a forward P/E near 10x, the market is giving you a discount here on a company sitting right in the path of a structural gas supercycle set to benefit from AI data center buildout.

Expand Energy, along with its neighbor to the North, Tourmaline Oil (TRMLF), is my top natural gas stock pick. I view both as premier, high-quality producers with relatively low risk compared to peers. Both may not seem deeply undervalued, but valuations compare favorably to peers, and I believe investors are truly buying into quality companies that command and deserve a valuation premium.

I view EXE stock as a BUY around $100 per share.

data.title: Hartford Municipal Opportunities ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US fixed-income markets delivered positive total returns in the fourth quarter of 2025 as policy uncertainty, fiscal developments, and divergent central bank actions shaped the investment landscape. The US began the quarter with its longest government shutdown on record, which delayed key economic data and forced the Federal Reserve (Fed) to make policy decisions with limited visibility. The shutdown ended in mid-November, but data gaps persisted, adding to market uncertainty. US agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), investment-grade corporate and high-yield corporate bonds produced positive total returns. MBS and high yield outperformed duration1 -equivalent US Treasuries, while investment-grade corporate bonds underperformed as spreads2 widened. The Bloomberg Municipal 1-15 Year Blend (1-17) Index returned 1.41% during the quarter. The ratio of 10-year AAA general obligations (GOs) to 10-year Treasuries decreased from 69.1% to 66.7%, remaining below the long-term historical average of 86% over the last 20 years.*

Performance Summary

  • The Hartford Municipal Opportunities ETF outperformed the Bloomberg Municipal 1-15 Year Blend (1-17) Index during the quarter.
  • Duration and yield-curve positioning added to relative returns. The portfolio maintained an underweight in the front and belly of the curve and an overweight in longer maturities, which supported performance as shorter-maturity AAA municipal yields sold off and long yields rallied.
  • Security selection in high-yield healthcare and investment-grade industrial-development bonds also contributed modestly to relative returns.
  • Our underweight in investment-grade GO bonds partially offset the positive relative returns.

Positioning & Outlook

  • Most states ended fiscal year 2025 with revenues above forecast. Although they have been declining recently, strong reserves keep us positive overall.
  • Many municipal sectors have the ability to raise revenues quickly to address inflationary pressures. The more labor-constrained sectors such as healthcare and state and local governments will be more challenged if inflation persists, but local governments still have exceptional cash reserves, and hospitals are in the best financial position they have been in over the past 5 years.
  • Expect municipals to behave defensively in a downturn given their high quality and strong balance sheets.
  • Tax-exempts are somewhat attractive at the corporate tax rate vs. corporate bonds, particularly relative to their recent history and at longer maturities.
  • Valuations are fair to rich across the curve, with the long end presenting the most value.
  • Following a record-breaking year of issuance in 2025, we expect this trend to continue and to be met by continued demand. A sharp move higher in rates is likely to lead to outflows and a more challenging market environment.

*Source: Bloomberg

2 Spreads are the difference in yields between two fixed-income securities with the same maturity but originating from different investment sectors.

data.title: Commodity Price Watch: February 2026
data.description:
What is the overall trend in commodity prices?

Prices will be flat in 2026 for most globally traded industrial commodities. Crude oil will dip into mid-2026 on oversupply. Rising trade barriers will continue to be an important factor for buyers. The US led in 2025; now, the movement for 2026 is, to some extent, the rest of the world joining the party

As measured by the Materials Price Index (MPI) by S&P Global Market Intelligence, industrial materials prices bottomed in the second quarter of 2025 and will revisit those levels in the second quarter of this year.

For the fourth quarter of 2025, the MPI was 5% below its year-earlier level. Prices are returning to year-earlier levels, however, as the MPI for the second quarter of 2026 is forecast to be 0.4% below the previous year.

With pockets of oversupply in some areas and tight fundamentals in others, there is no overarching trend for industrial commodities. The most general summary is that 2026 spend will be quite similar to 2025, if the basket looks similar to the MPI, although country-specific tariffs will lift spend where the tariffs have gone up.

A relatively new and very important feature of commodity markets is extreme regionality. Historically, commodities were traded globally, and similarities outweighed differences between regions. Protectionism and logistics disruption have changed this global view. There is also variation across category fundamentals.

What is our forecast for energy-related commodity prices?

A global supply surplus averaging just under 1 million barrels per day for 2026 will continue to drive prices down over the year. In the shorter term, geopolitics is shifting prices. The first quarter of 2026 will see Brent crude prices averaging $63/barrel.

This is elevated relative to expectations because of tensions between the US and Iran leading to a risk premium. The most likely scenario is that the risk premium erodes as market fears of a supply disruption ease. This is far from certain, though, and some military intervention could send prices higher.

Venezuelan exports will grow modestly by the end of 2027 relative to the 2025 average, helping ease global prices marginally. India will reportedly stop importing Russian crude owing to a trade deal with the US, but Russian barrels will likely still find their way onto global markets.

What is our forecast for chemical prices?

The majority of pricing series in February experienced an upward revision in the near term compared with last month, largely driven by significantly higher spot crude oil pricing in recent weeks. The most notable example is with benzene and benzene derivatives, which makes sense given how closely benzene normally tracks crude oil.

Still, there are exceptions. The most pronounced one is North American ethylene pricing, which saw a significant downward revision because of upstream natural gas coming in softer than anticipated so far in 2026. In addition, some modest downward revisions were put in place for a few European markets because of an overabundance of supply.

What is our forecast for steel prices?

Steel will be oversupplied globally, but high import barriers in the US and the EU support elevated pricing. Prices rise in Asia, but slowly and from a low starting point. Supply is ample and idle capacity is rampant, so shortages are unlikely. Prices trend upward based on production restraint and rising protectionism. However, protectionism causes price weakness in net exporting markets.

Demand will remain subdued well into 2026. Expect demand for most end markets to be weak enough that it will not alone cause upward price pressure in the European Union, the United States, and mainland China.

There is stronger demand in India and Southeast Asia, but their expansion is insufficient to offset the wider weakness elsewhere. Mills are restraining output to limit surplus, leaving much capacity idle. Prices barely cover the cost of production in key regions such as East Asia, and mills often sell at a loss.

What is our forecast for nonferrous metal prices?

Aluminum prices remain elevated on a combination of tight supply and bullish financial speculation. Prices reached a four-year high at the end of January before retreating. We expect them to hold at high levels for the rest of the first quarter before starting to ease on improving supply, with speculators turning less positive on metals and taking profits.

These developments are becoming visible, with inventories ticking upward and financial positioning on exchanges turning more neutral. However, the return will be slow and anemic, rather than a sharp correction downward.

Copper prices remain elevated on a combination of tight supply, tariff uncertainty, a weaker US dollar and financial speculation. These drivers are largely unchanged from January’s report. However, the strength of upward price pressures has waned, with prices stepping down and cycling rather than pushing incessantly upward.

Risks remain elevated. Supply that is mostly balanced could turn negative on any supply reductions, becoming tied up in financing or tariff policies, which may change at short notice.

The Lunar New Year holiday should bring a lull in industrial demand and a temporary letup in speculatory activity, as mainland Chinese traders take a break, but adds to the uncertainty when they return.

With contributions from Keyla Goodno

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cloudflare: AI Traffic And Workers Driving Upside
data.description:
Cloudflare (NET) reported extremely strong results in the fourth quarter, leading the stock to rebound modestly from its recent drawdown. Current strength appears to be broad-based, with application services an area of particular strength. This is before tailwinds like AI inferencing workloads begin to have a material impact.

Concerns over AI disrupting software companies could weigh on the stock in the near term. This fear is misplaced in Cloudflare's case, though, due to the infrastructure-heavy nature of its business and the competitive advantages it has.

I previously suggested that the Cloudflare bubble had reinflated. While the stock is now up since then, there has been significant volatility and opportunities to enter at lower prices. I continue to believe that investors need to be prepared for a substantial drawdown, but emerging tailwinds likely mean that Cloudflare will provide strong returns going forward.

Market Conditions

Despite growing concerns about the potential impact of AI on software, the demand environment appears to be improving for most software companies, with AI and cybersecurity remaining areas of particular strength.

Cloudflare has stated a number of times in the past that it plans on investing through its income statement, holding its operating income near breakeven for the foreseeable future. As a result, the company's hiring should be a reasonable indicator of revenue growth. The number of Cloudflare job openings steadily increased over the past 12 months and potentially points towards stronger revenue growth going forward.

This is supported by the number of job openings mentioning Cloudflare in the job requirements. After a sharp pullback in late 2022, this metric has been steadily increasing in recent years.

While Cloudflare's business is accelerating and the demand environment is supportive, its share price has been collateral damage of the uncertainty that AI has created for software investors.

I believe that this fear is misplaced in Cloudflare's case, though, as it is more of an infrastructure company, operating an increasingly complex network. Cloudflare handles roughly 20% of all internet traffic and invested 14% of revenue in its network in the third quarter. The company's network currently has 449 Tbps capacity and a presence in over 330 cities.

While it may take time for the market to discriminate, it is application companies that are most at risk, particularly if they only offer a simple interface on top of a system of record. In addition, much of Cloudflare's business stands to benefit from increased utilization rather than being pressured by seat-based pricing.

Cloudflare Business Updates

Cloudflare's dominant position in handling internet traffic means that it is well positioned to capitalize on the growing importance of crawlers and bot management. The company is adapting its platform to protect and manage content in the AI age, which includes introducing a Pay Per Crawl revenue generator for content creators. In support of this, Cloudflare is acquiring Human Native for its data marketplace.

Cloudflare also plans on implementing solutions for agentic commerce. Payments between agents may benefit from a specifically designed currency that is instant, global, secure, and facilitates microtransactions. Cloudflare has launched the NET Dollar project, although it is likely to work with partners for regulatory reasons. NET Dollar is a programmable USD-backed stablecoin that is designed for agentic commerce and will also enable creators to be rewarded for original content. While I am not particularly bullish on this initiative, Cloudflare is positioned to drive adoption through developers.

The company also has partnerships with Coinbase (COIN), Visa (V), Mastercard (MA), and American Express (AXP) based around agent-to-agent payments. In addition, Cloudflare is contributing to open standards such as the Agent Payments Protocol (Google) and x402 to simplify the process of sending and receiving payments on the Internet.

Cloudflare's Workers platform continues to expand at a rapid pace, with AI inference a growth driver. The platform is also being used to build AI agents and full-stack applications. Cloudflare had over 4.5 million developers on its platform at the end of 2025 and stands to benefit if vibe coding leads to an increase in applications. Cloudflare plans on acquiring Replicate, an AI platform for developers, which will provide users of the Workers platform access to AI models.

Cloudflare's transition from product-led growth to enterprise sales continues, removing one of the few real sources of doubt about the company. This is demonstrated by the record number of customers spending in excess of 1 million USD annually that have been added in recent quarters. The company also closed its largest deal ever in terms of annual contract value (42.5 million USD per year) in Q4. Cloudflare's sales capacity is growing at the fastest rate since 2022, but despite this, Cloudflare's sales team achieved its highest quota attainment in the last 4 years in Q4.

The sales force transformation is most relevant to Cloudflare's SASE solution, which is now feature complete. It isn't really clear how this product is performing, but Cloudflare has suggested that there have been no significant changes in the competitive landscape.

Financial Analysis

Cloudflare generated 614.5 million USD revenue in the fourth quarter, an increase of 34% YoY, and new ACV increased nearly 50% YoY, the highest rate since 2021. While AI inferencing is growing rapidly, it is still a small portion of Cloudflare's business at the moment.

Large customers are an area of particular strength, with revenue from large customers (>100,000 USD annual spend) increasing 42% YoY in Q4. This is notable as Cloudflare's ability to transition to drive enterprise sales has been a question mark. A growing percentage of revenue is also coming through channel partners, although this is still a relatively immature part of the business relative to peers like Zscaler (ZS).

Cloudflare expects 620-621 million USD revenue in the first quarter, an increase of 29-30% YoY. For the full year, the company expects 2.785-2.795 billion USD revenue, implying 28-29% growth. Cloudflare is also targeting 5 billion USD annualized revenue by the end of 2028 (a little over 25% annual revenue growth). Guidance is likely to prove overly conservative, though, and I expect Cloudflare's growth to approach 40% in the second half of the year.

Expansion of Cloudflare's customer base has also picked up in recent quarters, with the company's largest customer cohorts an area of particular strength. Cloudflare also continues to drive healthy expansion within existing customers, supported by a product portfolio that now includes more than 55 revenue-generating products. Cloudflare's dollar-based net retention rate was 120% in Q4, up 1% sequentially.

Cloudflare's gross margin has ticked lower in recent quarters, which could be indicative of new product growth. In addition, paid traffic has increased relative to free user traffic, meaning more network expense is being allocated to COGS. While this makes sense from an accounting perspective, it reduces the interpretability of gross margin changes.

Cloudflare's non-GAAP operating profit was almost 90 million USD in the fourth quarter at a 14.6% margin. The company also generated almost 100 million USD free cash flow in the quarter. While Cloudflare's margins are generally improving, the company continues to balance profitability and growth, which could mean that increased investments limit further gains in the short term. The company's 4.1 billion USD cash position and positive cash flows position it to do this. Cloudflare has suggested that it is targeting 20%-plus operating margins and 25%-plus free cash flow margins long-term.

Conclusion

Cloudflare still has a high valuation, particularly in comparison to a number of its peers that have faced significant multiple compression in recent years. The breadth of Cloudflare's business and its robust financial performance make comparisons difficult, though. Cloudflare is seeing stronger AI tailwinds than network security peers and is better positioned to capitalize on the edge compute opportunity than CDN peers.

Cloudflare's competitive advantages have also become better appreciated by the market in recent years, which likely ensures the company maintains its valuation premium. There is significant downside risk if investor sentiment further sours, though, and there is no guarantee that the company won't continue to be dragged down by AI fears. Despite this, I believe that Cloudflare will offer solid double-digit returns for investors willing to hold through any near-term volatility.

data.title: BNY Mellon Worldwide Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
"As markets may face volatility with geopolitical, monetary, and tariff policy outcomes unfolding, we remain confident in the long-term growth trajectories and fundamental business strength across our portfolio."

Market Review

The MSCI World Index 3.12% in a volatile quarter, as better-than-expected corporate earnings propelled global equities despite several growing risks. The quarter's strong earnings season painted a picture of a resilient global economy, but concerns of a potential artificial intelligence (AI) bubble and a more cautious consumer dampened the outlook. Central bank policy continues to be accommodative while trade tensions cooled in the quarter, but both themes face uncertainty in the year ahead.

Volatility was heightened in the quarter with investors scrutinizing key performance themes, including AI investments and consumer resiliency. Many large technology companies reported strong revenue and earnings growth during the period and pledged to increase capital expenditures, as tremendous computing demand outstrips current supply. Beneficiaries of the AI boom broadened out across the U.S., Europe, and Asia to include semiconductor equipment, memory, and component providers who reported strong earnings. As a corollary to earnings results, a series of partnerships between OpenAI and public technology companies were announced throughout the quarter that totaled over $1 trillion in future spending on AI chips, datacenters, and cloud computing. Investor concerns arose around the circular nature of funding deals, increased debt burdens, and the uncertain return profile of these investments. Notably, strong earnings from bellwether NVIDIA resulted in a muted market response, suggesting that investors became more discerning of AI-related investments this quarter.

Outside of information technology, the industrials sector also benefited from continued data center construction and investments made to modernize the electric grid. Within the financials sector, major banks saw earnings growth driven by strong loan and advisory activity, while some information services providers came under pressure from perceived AI threats to their business. Retail-facing companies reported higher levels of consumer uncertainty as high interest rates delayed large purchase decisions, such as home renovation projects. Consumer reports highlighted an increasingly pronounced bifurcation, with higher-income consumers continuing to spend broadly while lower-income consumers sought out value and traded down. Companies have largely been able to absorb tariff costs to-date, but management teams warned that they will have to raise prices eventually to make up for increased expenses.

European earnings held up well and demonstrated the continent's ability to react and adapt to U.S. tariffs. The European Central Bank (ECB) and the Bank of England (BoE) began to cut rates early in the year, governments stepped in with large spending bills, and European companies trimmed costs and improved efficiency. In China, slower-than-expected economic growth continues under the strain of tariffs and an ailing real estate sector. However, China demonstrated its ability to successfully reroute goods to other markets as its trade surplus surpassed $1 trillion for the first time.

On trade policy, President Trump and Chinese Leader Xi met for their long-awaited meeting in South Korea and agreed to a truce that would pause tariffs for another year. In a further thawing of relations, the U.S. government approved the sale of scaled-down AI chips to China. President Trump also met Japan's new Prime Minister, Sanae Takaichi, whose political agenda is expected to expand fiscal policy, support wage growth to offset inflation, and focus on geopolitical security as it relates to military and AI spending. Japanese equities rallied on the prospect of these pro-growth policy positions. While trade tensions cooled, the oscillating nature of tariff negotiations and a looming U.S. Supreme Court decision remain a risk.

Many of the global central banks arrived at the conclusion of their current easing cycle during the fourth quarter. The ECB held rates steady and the BoE implemented a single 25 bp cut in the quarter. Both agencies signaled they are at the end of their easing cycles, which commenced at the start of the year to deal with U.S.-imposed tariffs. In the U.S., inflation remained above 2%; however, the low-hiring job market and unemployment rate increase to 4.6% prompted the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) to cut the Federal Funds rate by 25 bps both in October and December. The Fed believes the current Federal Funds rate range of 3.50-3.75% is relatively neutral and neither supportive nor a hindrance to growth, signaling a potential pause before further easing is warranted. The loosening of financial conditions successfully buttressed the world's major economies against the impact of U.S. tariffs, but investors may not be able to rely on policy support going forward.

Investors paid greater attention to the multitude of risks this quarter, including a potential AI bubble, deteriorating employment data, and prolonged uncertainty over tariffs. Despite these challenges, strong corporate earnings drove global equity gains

Performance Review

The Fund trailed the Index in the period, driven by an overall negative stock selection effect. The Fund benefited from a positive stock selection effect within the consumer discretionary and communication services sectors, resulting in positive relative results. Within the real estate sector, a favorable underweight allocation supported relative performance.

Conversely, the Fund's disadvantageous stock selection across the industrials, information technology, and financials sectors detracted from relative performance.

Top Contributors

The top contributors to relative performance include Alphabet (GOOGL), LVMH (LVMHF), Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), AstraZeneca (AZN), and Apple (AAPL).

Top Detractors

The top detractors from relative performance include Microsoft (MSFT), BAE Systems (BAESF)(BAESY), Nintendo (NTDOF)(NTDOY), Meta Platforms (META), and Eaton Corp. (ETN).

Market Outlook

The Fund's investment philosophy remains consistent during periods of political and economic transformation. Governments across the world are rewriting economic and foreign policies and transforming trade relationships that have anchored stability in the preceding decades. These changes have resulted in unprecedented uncertainty and volatility. Harking back on our decades of investment experience, especially during uncertain or volatile markets, we remain confident in the industry-leading businesses that comprise our portfolio. We believe long-term value is driven by companies that can consistently grow earnings through various business cycles. We will continue to stay vigilant and invest in accordance with our investment philosophy, as we have done during previous periods of transformation.

data.title: Genpact: ATS Growth And Margin Expansion Strengthen The Investment Case
data.description:
Genpact Limited (G) is continuing to execute well against its ambition, and the results in the last fiscal year are proof that my confidence in the business is not misplaced. To be fair, the narrative shift has taken some patience to fully support, and I don’t think the market is fully onboard yet.

However, I’d say that the waiting phase is over, and the business now looks measurably different from a few years ago. As such, I’m reiterating my Buy stance on the stock.

FY2025 Results: Pivot Now in Numbers

Full-Year FY2025 Snapshot

Operating Cash Flow

First, revenue isn’t growing at a breakneck pace, but we must remember that this is a company that is shifting towards higher-value technology and AI-led services, so the topline will take some time to reflect that. For me, the mix shift and margin expansion are more important, and they’re not accidental. I’ll discuss them in a bit more detail shortly, but management explicitly tied the margin expansion to a higher-value ATS mix and disciplined cost execution during the Q4 FY25 earnings call.

Q3 2025 Revenue Breakdown

When we look at the figures from Q3 2025 and Q4 2025, it’s obvious that ATS is driving over half of Genpact’s revenue growth, and the company’s revenue per headcount is rising. In addition, its incremental revenue is coming in with structurally higher gross margins. These are all good signs, and that’s how operating leverage will continue compounding.

Besides that, I must highlight the company’s agentic layer. In 2025 alone, Genpact closed over $200 million in total contract value (TCV) just for its AP agentic solutions, according to the CEO, Balkrishan Kalra. That is impressive in itself, but my main takeaway is that more than 40% of that came from new clients. These aren’t pipeline deals that the company is expecting to materialize. It already has them booked, and at this rate, I believe that its agentic offerings will soon comprise a larger share of the revenue total than we expect. Given that I was already bullish on ATS growth back in September, seeing the segment ending the year with $1.2 billion in revenue and compounding at 17% just reinforces my confidence.

During the same earnings call, Mr. Kalra also said this:

We delivered over $5.5 billion in new bookings with healthy growth in Advanced Technology Solutions, which now account for more than 1/3 of total bookings. We won 16 large deals and continue to make progress with the next generation of market disruptors. We are in a very strong position as we enter 2026. Demand is healthy and growing.

ATS makes up around $1.8 billion of the company’s bookings for the year, which isn’t something to sneeze at. It means we might see it grow by another 17-22% in FY26, even if it doesn’t touch $1.8 billion in revenue. That is still exponential growth for the segment, and these developments have me convinced that we’re looking at a potential game-changer for Genpact’s business in the near future.

The Mix Shift is Not Slowing Down

I don’t see anything in the 2026 guide that suggests the pivot is stalling. In fact, I think this level of performance is going to become the new baseline.

Management guided to at least 7% revenue growth for FY2026, which is slightly higher than FY2025’s growth rate. They also expect high-teens growth in ATS, roughly 50 basis points of gross margin expansion, and about 10% adjusted EPS growth. I think that’s a bit of an undersell for what Genpact can do, but I also understand why they are being cautious with expectations.

Let’s look at the big picture here. ATS finished FY2025 at $1.204 billion, up 17%. If it compounds in the low twenties in 2026, we’re looking at something closer to the mid-$1.4B mark by the end of 2026. That pushes the segment toward a much larger share of total revenue without any heroics. Meanwhile, the company still has its Core Business Services (CBS) segment in a strong place. For reference, it grew by 3.7% in 2025, so if it can maintain this level, that will fund the transition.

I believe that combination is powerful, especially because you don’t have a shrinking legacy base that is dragging on consolidated growth. There’s a stable base and an accelerating, higher-margin layer sitting on top of it.

With the margins, it’s more of the same. Genpact’s gross margin has been inching closer to 36% for the last few years, and it finally cracked the ceiling in Q3 2025. That was a 36.4% quarter, and Q4 was 36.6%. FY2025 gross margin reached 36.0%, up 60 basis points, and the word from management is that they expect it to step up to roughly 36.5%. It’s a direct translation from incrementally better revenue to structurally better economics, and I like the pattern.

I discussed the bookings and agentic TCV already, so I won’t dwell on them here. My confidence in Genpact is based on the fact that if the company keeps delivering as expected, then its earnings power will look materially different two years from now than what most investors still assume today.

Priced Like a Steady Outsourcer

This is where I think the market is lagging. In my opinion, Genpact trades at a multiple that still reflects its historical profile more than its emerging one. On its FY2025 adjusted EPS of $3.66, the stock trades at a ~10x forward multiple. That is not commensurate with what I’d deem reasonable for a company that is guiding to 7% revenue growth, 10% EPS growth, and expanding margins. Besides, that multiple is less than half its sector median, which is around 24x.

We can also look at its EV/EBITDA profile. Again, it trades at a near-50% discount to the median, and my DCF model from my first article on the stock indicates that it should be closer to $50/share today in a bear case scenario with only 3% annual revenue growth. Bear in mind that I created that model when the stock was at ~$44/share, and my bull case only had it growing at 5% every year.

Now that it’s at 7% annually with even better prospects than at the time, it makes sense to assume that the stock is worth a lot closer to $60 today. Again, you’re not paying a premium multiple for high-teens ATS growth. You’re paying something closer to a steady BPO multiple, even though nearly a quarter of the company’s revenue now comes from higher-value technology-led services.

To me, that asymmetry is even more obvious, given the business’s trajectory.

There Are Risks

As with any position I take, I want to highlight some of the risks. ATS growth is a significant lever for my thesis, and if it slows from the high-teens pace we’ve seen, that will affect Genpact’s margins. So, the margin expansion story will lose momentum quickly.

That leads me to the second thing: how it executes with agentic AI. The news around the company’s TCV and bookings on this front is fantastic, but it is one thing to win the contracts and another thing to scale its delivery profitably across the larger enterprises. I believe Genpact is doing well at this point, but it is also possible that we might hear about some issues with its implementation or ROI complaints from clients. That would be far from ideal, and it directly affects revenue growth and margin expansion.

Finally, we must consider other macro conditions. The company serves global enterprises across financial services, consumer goods, and healthcare. These are fairly robust industries, yes, but they are also affected by various economic factors. As such, if they decide to cut down on their discretionary tech spend or scrutinize their costs, they might delay some of those transformation projects. Genpact has a legacy base that can keep it stable for a bit, but the fact remains that it is exposed to broader corporate spending cycles.

Another Buy, With Even More Conviction

I’ve outlined my view of Genpact’s business and its tailwinds, and I’m not waiting for the market to fully reprice the stock. It’s a good buy for all the reasons I listed above, and I’m confident that the stock will be a good value holding over the next few years.

data.title: Atlassian: Trust Growth Acceleration And RPO Buildup (Upgrade)
data.description:
In the span of just a few short weeks, software stocks have gone from being prized for potential AI tailwinds to being discarded for fear of being replaced by AI. The speed of the selloff is what has jarred mainstream investors, with many high-profile leading software businesses erasing years of gains in a very compressed period.

Few stocks have been as challenged as Atlassian (TEAM), the software giant known for its Jira IT management and collaboration tools. Atlassian has lost ~40% of its value since the start of the year alone, and over the past year, the stock has shed about two-thirds of its value, wiping off billions in market cap. The question for investors now is, is Atlassian’s future truly jeopardized by AI, or is this a clear signal of an oversold stock that should be bought?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Atlassian in November, when the stock was trading near $160 per share. Needless to say, Atlassian has been a major hole in my portfolio, and I did not expect such massive volatility. And yet, when I take a step back and assess the stock soberly now, it’s evident that the recent crash is almost entirely due to sentiment rather than due to fundamentals. Atlassian was one of the earliest software stocks to begin correcting, with shares already souring midway through 2025 on fears that vibe coding would lead to an erosion of the software developer jobs that constitute the company’s core paid seat count. This doomsday scenario has not happened, and I’m happy to take the opportunity to dollar cost average my position downward and buy Atlassian at decimated multiples of revenue and earnings. With this in mind, I’m upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy."

To me, there are several indicators that clearly show that Atlassian’s actual business remains quite secure and in growth mode, despite recent selloff fears. These are the core catalysts that investors need to be paying attention to, rather than the dour headlines:

  • Revenue growth is accelerating. Many software companies have said AI is a tailwind for their businesses, Atlassian included. Atlassian is one of the few companies that is actually showcasing accelerated revenue growth, which puts real data behind that assertion. I think the idea that vibe coding completely kills engineering jobs is a falsehood: vibe coding has emerged as a great way to create demos and proofs of concept, but fully ironclad applications will still at least need senior engineers to review code and to manage product development.
  • RPO growth is leading well ahead of revenue growth. Atlassian’s >40% growth in RPO, or essentially backlog, is a great indicator that its customers are committing to spending and building with Atlassian for the long haul, not cutting spending ahead of software engineer reductions.
  • Gross margin buildup. Atlassian’s gross margins in the high 80%s are unrivaled for the software industry and enable tremendous economies of scale. Many tech companies have seen AI infrastructure costs erode their gross margin profiles, but clearly this isn’t a concern for Atlassian, whose margins are still rising.

My sense of immediacy in buying Atlassian now, and my upgrade of the stock to a "Strong Buy" rating that represents a high degree of conviction I have in few stocks, is underscored by Atlassian’s bargain basement valuation. At current share prices near $84, Atlassian trades at a market cap of just $22.05 billion. Netting off the $1.78 billion of cash and strategic investments on Atlassian’s latest balance sheet against $988.6 million of debt gives us an enterprise value of $21.26 billion.

For FY26 (the year for Atlassian ending in June 2026), the company has upped its guidance to 22% y/y revenue growth and a 25.5% pro forma operating margin, maintaining margin expectations but raising growth by 120bps versus a previous expectation of 20.8% growth. This point cannot be understated: despite weakening sentiment, Atlassian continues to deliver beat-and-raise quarters.

For next year, FY27, Street expectations are calling for $5.51 in pro forma EPS (16% growth) on $7.50 billion in revenue (+18% y/y). This positions Atlassian’s forward multiples at:

  • 2.8x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 15.3x FY27 P/E
  • 14.8x FY27 ex-cash P/E

Recall that this is a stock that, not long before sentiment started turning against it, traded at double-digit revenue multiples and P/E multiples too high to justify. Even in today’s stock market after the sharp software selloff, there are few enterprise software businesses that have Atlassian’s “Rule of 40” execution and yet still trade at valuations below the S&P 500.

Take advantage of this extraordinary dip as a buying opportunity.

Q2 Download

Let’s now go through Atlassian’s Q2 (December quarter) results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Atlassian’s revenue grew 23% y/y to $1.59 billion, beating Wall Street’s expectations of $1.54 billion (+20% y/y). And not only did Atlassian beat Street figures, but we note, as shown in the chart below, that the company’s revenue growth accelerated two points versus 21% y/y growth in Q1.

None of these indicators point to a company that is facing any threat at all from AI eating software jobs. Consistently strong performance in the cloud, which is Atlassian’s principal deployment method, was driven not just by customer growth but also by paid seat expansion at existing customers. Per management’s commentary in the Q2 shareholder letter:

Strong enterprise sales momentum and increasing customer commitment to the Atlassian Platform drove RPO growth of 44% to $3.8 billion and current RPO growth of 30% to $2.7 billion in the quarter. In Q2, we closed a record number of deals greater than $1 million in ACV, up nearly 2x year-over-year, reflecting the compelling value our AI-powered System of Work delivers to customers [...]

Paid seat expansion, cross-sell of additional products, and customer retention all exceeded our expectations. Migrations, realized pricing, and upsell to higher value editions were in line with our expectations.

CEO Michael Cannon-Brookes also went on to say on the Q2 earnings call that the company found that its customers who use AI/vibe coding tools have 5% greater monthly active users and task creation activities in Jira than those who don’t.

Another strong indicator: Atlassian’s RPO (backlog) grew 44% y/y to $3.8 billion. This is a clear indication that customers aren’t afraid to commit to long-term projects and spending on Jira.

Atlassian didn't slack on profitability either. As shown in the chart below, pro forma operating margins rose 1 point y/y to 27%.

Stacked on top of 23% top-line growth, Atlassian has a "Rule of 40" score of 50 - representing a fantastic balance of growth and profitability that isn't commensurate with the stock's bargain multiples.

Risks and Key Takeaways

Of course, there are certainly also risks that we need to keep in mind here. Regardless of the impacts of AI, Atlassian is facing a weak macroeconomy, and many companies are tightening their budgets, particularly on IT. The company has achieved seat expansion so far, but if the macro tightens in 2026, we could begin to see evidence of more churn playing out. We note as well that Atlassian plays in a very competitive environment for workflow tools, stacked up against the likes of monday.com (MNDY) and Asana (ASAN) - all of whom are facing their own demand scares and share price erosion. In this environment, competitors could be more aggressive to cut prices.

That being said, I think risks are more than priced in at current valuation multiples. Take advantage of this dip to enter into Atlassian at a de-risked price.

data.title: Travel + Leisure: Positive Momentum Continues
data.description:
Shares of Travel + Leisure (TNL) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining nearly 40%, and investors received more good news Wednesday when the company reported solid quarterly results. The recurring revenue nature of the timeshare business has insulated it from pressures in travel spending, while its high-quality customer base has made credit quality comparatively resilient. I last covered TNL in November, reiterating the stock as a “Buy,” and since then it has gained a further 22%, justifying my bullishness. With shares past my $70 price target and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit TNL to see if the good news is now fully priced in.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Travel + Leisure earned $1.83 per share, which beat estimates by a penny as revenue grew 6% to $1.03 billion. Adjusted EBITDA was up 8% to $272 million, thanks to ongoing margin gains from the healthy utilization of its resorts. Additionally, TNL is undertaking a “resort optimization initiative” where it will exit underperforming or more seasonal resorts with newer resorts and locations. This resulted in a one-time charge of $216 million in Q4, though there should be ~$20 million of run-rate benefits to EBITDA going forward given reduced costs.

VOI business drives growth

Within its vacation ownership business, revenue grew 8% to $875 million, driving a 14% increase in EBITDA to $252 million. Sales of vacation ownership interest, or “VOI,” (this is the term the industry uses for timeshares), were up 8% to $638 million. It had 5% more tour attendees, and its volume per guest (“VPG”) was up 2%. These are both healthy metrics. More customers taking tours speaks to growing demand, and a higher conversion of these guests into sales speaks to a strong marketing and product offering.

TNL continues to benefit from having one of the higher-quality customer bases, helping to largely insulate it from affordability pressures on discretionary spending, which have limited results at other travel companies. Its average VOI owner has an income over $100k, and the FICO score of new buyers is solidly prime, having moved above 740. During 2025, it added more loan provisions, ending the year at 20.7%, up from 19.3% a year ago. Delinquencies are within expectations, and I expect this to continue, given my view that a recession is unlikely in 2026. With stable credit trends, this should migrate back towards 20% in 2026, providing some incremental free cash flow. Given the underlying credit quality of its owners, I am comfortable with existing loss reserves.

Aside from VOI sales, property management revenue was up 6% to $222 million, while financing revenue was up 1% to $116 million. With a growing membership and strong sales activity, we should see these revenue streams continue to provide revenue growth, and I expect mid-to-upper single-digit growth from the vacation ownership segment in 2026.

Its travel and management segment continues to face some pressures, with revenue down 6% to $148 million. As a result, EBITDA was down 10% to $47 million, and this exchange business has been struggling for some time. While transactions were up 3%, revenue per transaction fell by 9% due to an ongoing mix shift towards travel club transactions. I see this business facing long-term pressure as consumers continue to move to the newer, more flexible VOI model. Still, it will continue to provide free cash flow for some time. Whereas its vacation ownership segment drives growth, this is a legacy cash cow operation.

Cash flow enables ongoing capital returns

During 2025, it generated $516 million of free cash flow. Importantly, the company has a solid balance sheet with $3.5 billion of corporate debt and $253 million of cash (note that this debt excludes the $2.1 billion of nonrecourse securitizations that support its VOI receivables). Net leverage is 3.1x, a reasonable place for a business with recurring cash flow to operate.

As a result, it can return free cash flow to investors. Last year, TNL returned $449 million to shareholders via dividends and buybacks. Its share count is down 7.4% over the past year thanks to these repurchases. Alongside results, TNL announced a new $750 million buyback program. Plus, it plans to raise its quarterly dividend to $0.60, giving shares a 3.2% forward yield. As you can see, TNL has aggressively returned capital to investors during its time as a public company.

Outlook points to further upside

Looking forward, TNL expects to generate $1.03-$1.055 billion of EBITDA in 2026, up over 5% from last year. Underpinning this, it expects to grow VOI sales by 1%-5%; however, we are likely to see a small decline in VPG. TNL is making a concerted effort to boost sales to new owners (vs. existing owners seeking to upgrade). First-time buyers tend to buy lower-cost packages, but this can create a pipeline of future sales as they seek to upgrade over time.

Back in November, I was targeting ~$5 of run-rate capital returns per share. With the ongoing strength in sales and quality of its loan book, this view feels increasingly conservative. I see TNL positioned to return about $400 million of cash to investors, especially with leverage nearing 3.0x. This points to closer to ~$6 of capital returns. Given the rally in shares, we are unlikely to see the share count fall quite as dramatically, but a ~5% reduction from buybacks is well within the realm of possibility.

Shares remain a "Buy"

At ~$75, shares are now trading at an 8% capital return yield. Even after their large run, shares are still not that expensive. That reflects just how cheap TNL was trading a few months ago when there was excessive fear about credit losses on its receivables portfolio. While funded with nonrecourse debt, worse performance can limit how much residual value is left for TNL in these structures. Given its best-in-breed customer base and healthy balance sheet, we could see TNL move towards a 7% free cash flow, or $85, though I view this as the upper limit of valuation.

After this solid quarter and outlook, I see no rush to exit TNL, especially with a growing dividend and benefit from repurchase activity. I expect shares to migrate to $80-$85 over the next year. Combined with its dividend, that leaves sufficient upside to remain a “Buy.” While I believe the majority of the upside has occurred, TNL can continue to deliver strong performance in 2026.

data.title: KLA Corporation: I Would Take Some Profits (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in early September 2025, I reiterated my hold rating on KLA Corporation (KLAC). Despite overall resilient results and robust cash flows, there was notable top line growth deceleration in their segments. Forward-looking guidance seemed disappointing, and I concluded that the valuation was around fair. In hindsight, I was probably too conservative on KLA stock, as the chart below clearly shows that there have been monstrous gains since the publication of my previous update. Regardless, today I hope to offer a new in-depth analysis for readers and see if my rating needs any revising.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Q2 was a mixed quarter for KLA. Top-line growth slowed despite memory tailwinds, but margins showed expansion. Moving forward, guidance for Q3 is also mixed, and the company's exposure to China remains a very real risk to their business. With the forward P/E very near multiyear highs, there is room for the valuation to correct. I believe investors should now take some profits even if they aren't exiting, and so I'm downgrading KLA stock to a sell.

Growth Continues To Moderate

While the stock has advanced mightily, KLA's top line growth seems to be headed in the opposite direction. For FY2026 Q2, the company posted total revenues worth $3.3 billion, up 7.12% YoY. While this is still quite a respectable increase, you can see above that this represents the third consecutive quarter of deceleration. Therefore, the business as a whole has become a bit sluggish in my view.

Given the unrelenting demand for semiconductors and the stock's performance in recent months, I honestly expected more from their top-line numbers. Analysts would disagree, as they actually still beat expectations by $46.47 million. Nonetheless, first impressions are that momentum seems a bit weak.

Memory Is A Key Driver

In the year-ago quarter, memory made up just 24% of KLA's semiconductor process control segment's revenue. As you can see on the right, the importance of memory has increased significantly in the past year, as it now makes up 40% of the segment's revenue. By now, it is no secret that the tech sector is facing a memory chip supply crunch as AI data centers have created unprecedented demand. Many sectors, like automotive and consumer electronics, could feel the effects, and so it's quite intuitive that chip manufacturers are looking to up production.

For KLA, that is clearly a tailwind, and the increasing revenue contribution from their memory business is a good indication of this. For the semiconductor industry as a whole, in 2025, logic product sales growth of 39.9% outpaced memory product growth of 34.8%. Moving forward, I would say memory chips will be more important than ever, as total semiconductor sales are projected to reach $1 trillion in 2026. I expect memory to continue being a leading driver for KLA's growth, and contribution to revenue is likely to continue to rise as a result of these tailwinds.

China Still A Key Risk

In the introduction, I mentioned that KLA has decreased their reliance on China. That was the case in their Q2 results as well. In the prior year period, 36% of their revenue came from the Chinese market, while for FY2026 Q2, 30% of revenues came from the world's second largest economy. While the reduced reliance definitely helps with their business' geopolitical risk, I would say that they are still at the mercy of such headwinds should they arise.

At the end of December, it was reported that Chinese regulators are mandating chipmakers to use 50% domestic equipment. For a company like KLA that has significant exposure to China, I would say that these developments are well worth monitoring for investors. They're growing less reliant, but if U.S. - China relations sour in the near future, KLA may still be in for a blow that can't be easily mitigated.

Margins & Bottom Line Performance

While top-line growth has moved in an unfavorable direction, the company's margins have expanded to soften the blow. For adjusted gross margin, there has been YoY expansion from 61.7% to 62.6% as a sign that their pricing power remains solid and that cost controls are strong.

As for operating margin, there was a 50 basis point expansion to 42.8%. Since this improvement is smaller than the gross margin improvement, there may have been some slight expense control pressures, but nonetheless, expansion in both gross and operating margin can't really be complained about.

At the end of the day, their adjusted EPS of $8.85 represented 7.93% growth. This bested the top line growth rate and analyst expectations, and so profitability for KLA is not something worth worrying about right now based on these results.

Do Capital Returns Signal Caution?

One of the financial highlights for KLA in Q2 was their FCF growth. As you can see above, they reported FCF of $1.26 billion, and that represents an impressive growth rate of 66.67% YoY. This is a multiyear high by a good margin. Furthermore, FCF margin improved from 25% to 38% YoY, while FCF conversion improved from 69% to 108%. These are all signals that cash generation by their business has been rock solid. However, the company doesn't seem overly eager to share the cash with shareholders.

While FCF increased sharply YoY, share repurchases for the quarter dropped from $650 million to $548 million even as total dividend payout increased from $227 million to $250 million. As a whole, capital returns are down YoY, and could that be a signal of cautiousness? Maybe. The fact that they chose to reduce stock buybacks may signal an unattractive valuation, and so investors should keep that in mind.

Quarterly Outlook

For their FY2026 Q3, they expect revenues to be at around $3.35 billion, and this was above analyst consensus. That implies a growth rate of 9.5% YoY and would snap the run of consecutive top-line growth decelerations. Therefore, there are signs of a reaccelerating business. That was the main point of weakness in Q2, and it seems they'll see their fortunes improve.

For gross margin, they are expecting 61.75% at the midpoint. This would actually be an unfavorable change from the year-ago quarter's 63%, and so the cost control strength from Q2 won't continue. Lastly, for adjusted EPS, their $9.08 midpoint would be an 8.0% increase, and this was also above consensus. This growth rate would be nearly unchanged from Q2.

As a whole, I would say that guidance is a little mixed. Q2 results were mixed, with growth slowing but margins expanding. Funny enough, for Q3, growth is expected to reaccelerate, but margin may be under pressure. At the end of the day, the bottom line growth will be nearly unchanged, and so I conclude that the outlook is respectable but also some distance from being perfect.

Valuation Seems Rich

Despite retreating a bit from the high recently, KLA's forward P/E ratio is still at one of the highest levels in the past few years. I believe the valuation may be a bit rich given the fundamentals we have discussed today.

In this analysis, it was shown that the company's opportunity in memory is large, but there are some challenges moving forward. As stated above, EPS growth is expected to remain in just the single digits, with the company likely to see margin contraction moving forward. Furthermore, the possibly largest risk to KLA's business is their still significant exposure to the Chinese market. The regulatory environment there is growing a bit hostile and can be unpredictable.

Given their opportunity to continue benefitting from AI tailwinds but also the notable challenges and risks the company is facing, I would say that a forward P/E near multiyear highs is a little bit on the optimistic side of things. In my view, there is room for a pullback in the valuation, as there may be some memory-related froth here.

Conclusion

At the current valuation, I would say that KLA has quite a bit of good news priced in. If everything goes to plan and risks don't materialize into headwinds, the stock could perform just fine. However, when considering the distribution of outcomes, I would say there are reasons to be a bit cautious at current levels. The stock price strongly captures the opportunity in AI, particularly in memory, but may have overlooked some other less bullish factors. At this point, I believe investors would be wise to partially take profits, and so I have decided to downgrade KLA to a sell rating.

data.title: Janus Henderson Securitized Income ETF Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned 1.62% and the ICE BofA US ABS & CMBS TR returned 1.21%.

Contributors/detractors

Security selection within agency collateralized mortgage obligations ('CMO') and commercial mortgage-backed securities ('CMBS') contributed. Exposure to agency MBS and asset-backed securities ('ABS') detracted.

Outlook

A resilient economy, AI-driven investment, and anticipated Fed easing create a favorable backdrop for securitized fixed income. Active strategies focused on carry and risk-adjusted returns remain essential, in our view.

Investment environment

  • U.S. securitized markets delivered positive returns for the quarter. Short-term Treasury yields rallied, helping to drive performance. Securitized sectors broadly outperformed investment-grade ('IG') corporates and U.S. Treasuries.
  • In December, the Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates by 25 basis points (BPS) for a third consecutive meeting in response to a weaker jobs market, with the U.S. unemployment rate hitting a four-year high of 4.6% in November. Other data releases were more positive, with inflation dipping below expectations in November and third-quarter gross domestic product ('GDP') growth that surprised on the upside.
  • Speculation continued to build around who would replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell when his term expires in May and the degree to which their appointment might signal a shift in the central bank's traditional operational independence.
  • Spreads on IG ABS ended the quarter three basis points wider, at 52 bps, while spreads on IG CMBS ended unchanged, at 81 bps.

Portfolio review

Credit spreads continued to trade tight as investor sentiment remained upbeat amid strong corporate profitability, expected Fed easing, and robust economic growth supported by tax cuts and heavy investment in artificial intelligence ('AI'). While securitized spreads are trading around their cyclical tight levels, we believe securitized assets still are attractively priced relative to corporates as interest-rate volatility cools and consumer fundamentals remain strong.

Security selection contributed positively due, in part, to the fund's CMBS and agency CMO picks, but also thanks to the fund's greater diversification across securitized sectors compared with the benchmark. The fund's exposure to mortgages was particularly helpful to performance, as the mortgage sector ended the year on a high, helped by a steeper yield curve and lower interest rate volatility. Security selection within ABS detracted.

During the quarter, we increased our allocation to CLOs and MBS while trimming our exposure to CMBS. We took an interest, too, in data center-backed ABS, in line with the broader AI trend.

We continue to prefer the yield exposure at the short end of the yield curve, where investors may be better shielded from increases in term premiums on the long end due to potential concerns surrounding Fed independence, fiscal discipline, and foreign demand for U.S. Treasuries. We believe this view is further supported by the combination of forthcoming tax cuts, stubborn inflation, and upward revisions to economic growth estimates.

Manager outlook

While 2025 was an exceptional year for fixed income, we are similarly constructive on the outlook for 2026, albeit with a focus on earning carry rather than pursuing significant spread compression. In our view, current yield levels continue to present an attractive entry point for securitized fixed income strategies, supported by a constructive macroeconomic backdrop and favorable monetary policy trends.

U.S. economic growth is expected to remain positive, with real GDP projected at approximately 2.0% in 2026. While some sectors exhibit late-cycle characteristics, resilient consumer spending and corporate profitability – bolstered by AI adoption – should sustain economic momentum. Significant AI-driven investments in chips and data centers are anticipated to further fuel growth.

We think risks surrounding tariffs, immigration reform, and geopolitics are expected to moderate, while tax reforms under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act ('OBIBBA') may provide additional tailwinds. Inflation, despite disinflationary progress in energy and housing, is likely to remain above the Fed's target, in the 2.6% to 3.0% range, due to sticky services prices and delayed tariff effects.

Fiscal deficits and concerns over Fed independence may keep long-end yields rangebound. While uncertainty surrounds the Fed's near-term decisions, markets anticipate two to three rate cuts by year-end 2026. The Fed's pivot from quantitative tightening to quantitative easing – and its readiness to ease aggressively if growth falters – should support spreads. AI-driven productivity gains and tighter immigration policies could create a unique dynamic of strong growth alongside weaker labor markets, reinforcing the case for continued Fed easing.

Broadly speaking, key themes within the portfolio include our bullishness on credit spread carry amid robust corporate and consumer fundamentals and expected Fed cuts. Securitized assets are also attractively valued compared with corporates and offer a relatively stronger credit-quality mix. Furthermore, real yields are still above 1% and offer inflation-adjusted return potential.

A resilient economy, AI-driven investment, and anticipated Fed easing create a favorable backdrop for securitized fixed income in 2026. Active, diversified strategies focused on carry and risk-adjusted returns remain essential, in our view.

Portfolio management

John Kerschner, CFA

Definitions

10-year Treasury yield is the interest rate on U.S. Treasury bonds that will mature 10 years from the date of purchase.

Basis point (BP) equals 1/100 of a percentage point. 1 bp = 0.01%, 100 bps = 1%.

Carry is the excess income earned from holding a higher-yielding security relative to another.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between securities with similar maturity but different credit quality. Widening spreads generally indicate deteriorating creditworthiness of corporate borrowers, and narrowing indicate improving.

Duration measures a bond price's sensitivity to changes in interest rates. The longer a bond's duration, the higher its sensitivity to changes in interest rates and vice versa.

Quantitative Easing ('QE') is a government monetary policy occasionally used to increase the money supply by buying government securities or other securities from the market.

Quantitative Tightening ('QT') is a government monetary policy occasionally used to decrease the money supply by either selling government securities or letting them mature and removing them from its cash balances.

Volatility measures risk using the dispersion of returns for a given investment.

A yield curve plots the yields (interest rate) of bonds with equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Typically bonds with longer maturities have higher yields.

Fixed income securities are subject to interest rate, inflation, credit and default risk. The bond market is volatile. As interest rates rise, bond prices usually fall, and vice versa. The return of principal is not guaranteed, and prices may decline if an issuer fails to make timely payments or its credit strength weakens.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOS) are debt securities issued in different tranches, with varying degrees of risk, and backed by an underlying portfolio consisting primarily of below investment grade corporate loans. The return of principal is not guaranteed, and prices may decline if payments are not made timely or credit strength weakens. CLOs are subject to liquidity risk, interest rate risk, credit risk, call risk and the risk of default of the underlying assets.

Derivatives can be more volatile and sensitive to economic or market changes than other investments, which could result in losses exceeding the original investment and magnified by leverage.

Increased portfolio turnover may result in higher expenses and potentially higher net taxable gains or losses.

Actively managed portfolios may fail to produce the intended results. No investment strategy can ensure a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

Securitized products, such as mortgage- and asset-backed securities, are more sensitive to interest rate changes, have extension and prepayment risk, and are subject to more credit, valuation and liquidity risk than other fixed-income securities.
